_readme: This file defines every gauge on the Scorecard: its data source, its polarity (which direction counts as 'better'), and its weight in the composite verdict. Every polarity choice must be signed off by the site owner before use — see polarityJustification on each gauge. Phase C in progress: all 16 gauges configured (weights equally split 1/16, still a placeholder pending Phase D calibration). 'accessType: api' gauges are fetched by /pipeline (run `npm run pipeline`); 'accessType: manual' gauges are entered by hand via data/manual/ — see its README.md. Until a gauge has real data, its file keeps SAMPLE_DATA status (or doesn't exist yet) and the site shows it as sample/awaiting data, never silently as live. See CLAUDE.md and METHODOLOGY.md.
_directionBasisNote: Direction (improving/flat/deteriorating) shown everywhere on the site — cards, dot strips, What's Moving — is peer-relative: it classifies the trend in Australia's level SCORE (position vs the 9-country peer set), not the trend in the raw published number. The raw-value trend is still shown, separately, in the 'Two ways to read this' block on each gauge detail page. See METHODOLOGY.md.
_scoreBandsTodo: TODO(Phase D): the thresholds below (24/44/59/79) are placeholders picked for a clean 5-way split of 0-100. They have NOT been calibrated against real data and MUST be reviewed before any public release, once all 16 gauges are live with real numbers.

gauges.name: Living standards
gauges.polarityJustification: Higher GDP per capita, PPP-adjusted, means more real purchasing power per person — an unambiguous 'more is better' metric.
gauges.oneLiner: How much real economic output there is per person, adjusted for prices.

gauges.name: Productivity
gauges.unit: GDP per hour worked (USD, constant prices, 2015 PPPs)
gauges.polarityJustification: Higher output per hour worked means the economy generates more value for the same labour input — the standard productivity definition.
gauges.dataPolicy: OECD's live SDMX endpoint for this dataflow (DF_PDB_LV) redirects to an archive endpoint carrying the annotation NonProductionDataflow=true — OECD's own metadata flagging it as not intended for automated production queries. After three fix attempts (missing Accept headers, missing references=all on the redirect, a generic unhandled server exception on the archive endpoint itself) the query never returned data. Moved to a manual lane rather than continuing to debug infrastructure OECD itself has marked non-production — see CLAUDE.md for the full history. The same numbers are downloaded by hand from data-explorer.oecd.org using the template and instructions in data/manual/, then entered into data/processed/productivity.json directly. Not fetched automatically — update it by hand periodically.
gauges.oneLiner: How much economic value an hour of work produces.
gauges._seriesIdCorrection: The brief-assumed code (PDB_LV alone, via stats.oecd.org) is dead — OECD retired OECD.Stat / the old SDMX API in favour of a new SDMX API at sdmx.oecd.org with restructured agency/dataflow/version coordinates. Verified live 2026-07-14: the dataflow still exists as OECD.SDD.TPS,DSD_PDB@DF_PDB_LV,1.0, but is itself flagged NonProductionDataflow and redirects to an archive endpoint — see dataPolicy above and CLAUDE.md.

gauges.name: Education
gauges.unit: PISA mean score (maths, reading, science average)
gauges.polarityJustification: A higher average PISA score reflects stronger measured student performance across the three tested domains.
gauges.dataPolicy: PISA publishes three separate domain scores (reading, mathematics, science), never a pre-blended figure — this gauge's value is the average of the three, computed from OECD's own published per-domain numbers, not sourced pre-averaged. Cadence was 3-yearly through 2022; OECD is moving to a 4-year cycle from PISA 2025 onward (results due 8 Sept 2026) — staleAfterMonths is set to 45 (3.75 years) to give a realistic publication-lag grace period without nagging monthly for a source that only updates every 3-4 years. As of 2026-07-16, 2018 and 2022 are entered (real data, not fabricated back-years) — 2018 sourced from OECD PISA 2018 Results Volume I, Table I.1. A 2012/2015 backfill is still planned to extend trend history further; direction now computes from the real 2018→2022 movement (see the computeGaugeScore null-direction fix in lib/scoring.ts, 2026-07-16, which is what made a single-point gauge show 'no trend' honestly rather than a false 'flat' before this backfill existed).

source.seriesName: PISA mean score (maths + reading + science average)

gauges.oneLiner: How well 15-year-olds perform on a standardised international test.

gauges.name: Innovation
gauges.polarityJustification: Higher R&D spending as a share of GDP means more of the economy's resources are going toward developing new products, processes, and technologies.

source.seriesName: Research and development expenditure (% of GDP)

gauges.oneLiner: How much of the economy is invested in research and development.

gauges.name: External position
gauges._polarityDecision: SIGNED OFF by site owner, 2026-07-14: 'surplus = better' is a deliberate framework choice, not a claim of settled economics — see the Methodology page for the explicit caveat that this is contestable.
gauges.polarityJustification: This Scorecard treats external self-sufficiency — a stronger surplus / smaller deficit, i.e. less reliance on foreign capital — as the 'better' end of the scale. Economists legitimately debate current account polarity (a deficit can also reflect healthy foreign investment inflows); this is a stated framework choice, not an assertion that surpluses are objectively superior. See the Methodology page.
gauges.oneLiner: Whether the country is a net lender or net borrower to the rest of the world.

gauges.name: Rule of law & corruption
gauges.unit: WGI Rule of Law + Control of Corruption, averaged (estimate, -2.5 to +2.5)
gauges.polarityJustification: Higher WGI estimates mean stronger rule of law and lower perceived corruption — unambiguously better for governance quality.
gauges._seriesIdCorrection: The brief's original assumed codes (RL.EST, CC.EST) are dead on the current World Bank API — confirmed via live test call, they return 'indicator not found'. The correct current codes, verified live 2026-07-14, are GOV_WGI_RL.EST and GOV_WGI_CC.EST under World Bank source 3 (Worldwide Governance Indicators, last updated 2026-03-18). Documented per the brief's instruction to log series ID corrections here and in METHODOLOGY.md.

source.seriesName: Rule of Law & Control of Corruption, governance estimates

componentSeriesIds.name: Rule of Law - Governance estimate (approx. -2.5 to +2.5)
Control of Corruption - Governance estimate (approx. -2.5 to +2.5)

gauges.oneLiner: How strong legal institutions are and how well corruption is controlled.

gauges.name: Demographic momentum
gauges.polarityJustification: Faster working-age population growth means a larger future labour supply and growth potential — several peers in this set (Japan, South Korea, Germany) face the opposite problem of a shrinking working-age population straining growth and pensions, which this gauge is meant to surface.

source.seriesName: Population ages 15-64, total (pipeline derives year-over-year % growth from this level series)

gauges.oneLiner: How fast the working-age population is growing or shrinking.

gauges.name: Trade
gauges.unit: Share of world exports of goods & services (%)
gauges.polarityJustification: A larger share of world exports reflects greater economic weight and competitiveness in global trade — a size/influence metric, not a policy tradeoff like the external-position gauge.

source.seriesName: Exports of goods and services, share of world total (derived: country ÷ WLD aggregate)

gauges.oneLiner: How much of world trade in goods and services Australia accounts for.

gauges.name: Economic output
gauges.polarityJustification: A larger share of world GDP (PPP) reflects greater economic weight — a size/influence metric like Trade, not a policy tradeoff.
gauges.dataPolicy: IMF's World Economic Outlook mixes its own forward projections into the published series, with no machine-readable flag in this API to tell an actual from a projection. Policy: any year from the current calendar year onward is excluded, so a forecast is never presented on this site as an achieved fact.

maturityOverride.reason: IMF blocks GitHub Actions' IP range specifically — a confirmed standing limitation (see CLAUDE.md's "Pipeline environment quirks"). The unattended monthly pipeline can never refresh this gauge; only a manually-triggered local run can. Capped at Live rather than Established, since "survived a scheduled refresh" means the automated cron, not a human re-running it by hand.

source.seriesName: GDP based on PPP, share of world (World Economic Outlook)

gauges.oneLiner: How much of the world economy Australia accounts for, adjusted for prices.

gauges.name: Debt burden
gauges.unit: Household + government debt, % of GDP (BIS Total Credit)
gauges.polarityJustification: A lower combined household and government debt burden relative to GDP means less financial fragility and less exposure to a rise in interest rates or a credit shock.

source.seriesId: WS_TC (Total Credit), borrower sectors H+G summed
source.seriesName: Credit to households & NPISHs + credit to general government, % of GDP

gauges.oneLiner: How much combined household and government debt there is relative to the size of the economy.

gauges.name: Housing pressure
gauges.polarityJustification: A lower (or slower-rising) price-to-income ratio means housing is more affordable relative to earnings — higher values mean it takes more years of income to buy a home.

source.seriesName: Analytical House Price Indicators — price-to-income ratio

gauges.oneLiner: How expensive housing is relative to income.

gauges.name: Human capital depth
gauges.polarityJustification: A higher share of 25-34 year-olds with tertiary education means a more highly-skilled incoming workforce.
gauges.dataPolicy: OECD's SDMX API for this series returned no matching data after three attempts with different dimension keys (see CLAUDE.md for the full debugging history). Moved to a manual lane: the same numbers are downloaded by hand from data-explorer.oecd.org using the template and instructions in data/manual/, then entered into data/processed/human-capital-depth.json directly. Not fetched automatically, so it won't refresh on the monthly pipeline run — update it by hand periodically.

source.seriesName: Educational attainment distribution — tertiary, 25-34 year-olds, % of age group

gauges.oneLiner: What share of young adults have completed tertiary education.

gauges.name: Military capability
gauges.polarityJustification: Higher military spending relative to GDP is read here as capability, deterrence, and alliance credibility, not as militarization or opportunity cost — a genuine values choice, signed off by the site owner 2026-07-14, not a factual default. Could reasonably be flipped by a future site owner, but only as a deliberate re-decision, not a silent drift — see CLAUDE.md.
gauges.dataPolicy: SIPRI's Military Expenditure Database publishes spending in several forms (current/constant USD, per-capita, share of government spending, share of GDP). This gauge uses the 'Share of GDP' figures specifically, for consistency with this project's other normalized, cross-country-comparable gauges (external-position, trade, debt-burden) — not absolute spending, which would favour larger economies regardless of actual military priority. Fetched automatically: verified live 2026-07-14 that SIPRI publishes the full database as a direct .xlsx download with no login/API key, parsed by pipeline/lib/xlsx.mjs + pipeline/lib/sipri.mjs — see CLAUDE.md.
gauges.oneLiner: How much of the economy goes toward military spending.

gauges.name: Economic complexity
gauges.polarityJustification: A higher Economic Complexity Index reflects a more diversified, sophisticated export base with more embedded knowhow — the standard reading of ECI, and uncontroversial (unlike military capability or inequality, this polarity had no real open question).
gauges.dataPolicy: Fetched automatically via Harvard Growth Lab's public, unauthenticated GraphQL API (atlas.hks.harvard.edu/api/graphql) — verified live 2026-07-14 via schema introspection, not assumed from documentation. Country IDs are UN M49 numeric codes, resolved dynamically each run rather than hardcoded (pipeline/lib/harvardAtlas.mjs) — see CLAUDE.md.
gauges.oneLiner: How diversified and technologically sophisticated the economy's exports are.

gauges.name: Inequality
gauges.polarityJustification: A lower Gini coefficient means income is more evenly distributed across the population — the standard reading of income inequality.
gauges.dataPolicy: This gauge is scored from OECD's Income Distribution Database Gini coefficient only, signed off by the site owner 2026-07-14. The World Inequality Database's top-wealth-share figure is shown on this gauge's detail page as context (income and wealth inequality are genuinely different things) via GaugeData.contextSeries, but does not feed the composite score — keeps this gauge to one raw series like every other gauge on the site, rather than building new multi-component scoring machinery for this one case. Stays manual: OECD's SDMX endpoint for this dataflow returned a Cloudflare bot-protection page on 3/3 live attempts from this environment (2026-07-14) — rather than build a fetcher against a dataflow whose real structure was never verified, this is entered by hand via data/manual/. OECD's Gini data updates on a rolling basis with uneven lag across countries (some countries' most recent year may be 2-3 years older than others') — staleAfterMonths is set to 24 rather than the usual 15 to reflect that real, expected lag rather than treating it as always overdue. See CLAUDE.md for the full reasoning.

source.seriesName: Income Distribution Database — Gini coefficient, disposable income

gauges.oneLiner: How evenly income is distributed across the population.

gauges.name: Internal cohesion
gauges.unit: V-Dem political polarization score (interval scale, mean-centered at 0 across all country-years — not a 0-1 share)
gauges.polarityJustification: A lower score means society is less divided into hostile, antagonistic political camps — read as stronger internal cohesion. A higher score means more cross-camp hostility and discouraged interaction along ideological lines. Polarity flipped 2026-07-16 as part of the v2cacamps switch (see dataPolicy) — v2x_cspart, the prior series, was higher_is_better.
gauges.dataPolicy: Tracks V-Dem's Political polarization indicator (v2cacamps) — switched 2026-07-16 from the originally-chosen v2x_cspart (Civil Society Participation Index) after site-owner review found v2x_cspart measures civil-society consultation/participation, a different concept from the polarization/internal-order-and-disorder concept this gauge was actually specified for. v2cacamps directly matches the brief: V-Dem's own definition is 'the extent to which society is divided into hostile political camps' and political differences 'discourage interaction across ideological lines.' v2x_polyarchy and v2x_egaldem, the two alternatives weighed against v2x_cspart in the original (2026-07-14) decision, were never polarization measures either and remain correctly rejected — see CLAUDE.md for the full reversal history and why the original candidate set never included a polarization variable at all. Scale note: unlike v2x_cspart and every other V-Dem series on this site, v2cacamps is a raw, single-question expert-survey component, not one of V-Dem's smoothed 0-1 'v2x_' aggregate indices — its published values are an interval-converted score, mean-centered at 0 across all country-years (roughly -4 to +4 in practice, 0 = global average polarization), not a bounded share. Coverage verified live 2026-07-16 (V-Dem data via Our World in Data): all 9 peer countries have published values from at least 1900 through 2025, none missing from the indicator. Being a single-question component rather than a multi-indicator aggregate, a country's value can repeat unchanged across consecutive years between expert re-coding rounds — real data, not a gap, but disclosed here since every other gauge on this site updates more granularly. Stays manual: V-Dem's actual CSV download is gated behind a registration form (no direct URL), and the only freely-fetchable file (via V-Dem Institute's own GitHub org) is a 33MB R binary (.RData) with no safe dependency-free parsing path. Entered by hand via data/manual/.
gauges.oneLiner: How divided a society has become into hostile, antagonistic political camps.
